VO_EN: You put them in the back of your car.
The radio sits on your belt.
The precinct smells like burnt coffee and floor cleaner.
The badge still catches the light when you move.
You see a cop walk someone past the booking desk.
Past the terminal where you log arrests.
Through a door with no label on it.
The cop doesn't come back out.
You stand there holding a cup you've already finished.
Next day you ask the senior cop.
The kind of look that means the conversation's over before you finished asking.
No one says it in front of the captain.
Haven't walked anyone through that door.
But you know where it leads.
You eat lunch in the break room on a Tuesday and nobody mentions it.
Guy's belligerent but the paperwork would take four hours and you haven't slept since yesterday morning.
You turn left instead of right.
The door handle's cold in your hand.
Inside there's a chair and a table and a fluorescent light that flickers once when you flip the switch.
Before that you ask them one thing.
During the drive their phone rang three times.
Three times and then it went quiet.
You turned it off before the fourth.
Put it in your jacket pocket.
Small and warm against your ribs.
You can feel it through the lining.
The desk sergeant's typing something when you walk by.
Two days later a friend calls the precinct looking for someone.
You hear the desk sergeant across the room.
No one by that name was brought in.
You lean back in your chair and you exhale and for a second the whole precinct feels like any other precinct on any other night.
The fluorescent light above the desk hums.
That night you check the booking system.
You close the screen and open it again.
The second time it's not easier.
You're not testing the system anymore.
You start testing who disappears cleanly.
Person with a phone that keeps ringing.
Person who mentions a job in the morning.
Person who looks like someone's waiting for them at home.
You sit in the patrol car after and tap the dashboard once.
You watch the process like you've never seen it before and for a moment it feels unfamiliar - all that procedure for one person who said yes.
You hear the sergeant at the desk.
The client is in the room.
You can hear the lawyer's voice through the receiver from where you're standing.
The lawyer calls the next day.
The client stops asking for the phone.
You walk past the room on your way to the back lot.
The vending machine at the end of the hall is out of everything except water.
You check it the way other people check the weather.
You keep the number in your head the way you'd keep a balance in a checking account - something you monitor but don't talk about.
You adjust it when something changes.
You check the room before your shift one morning.
Or the room has another user now.
You close the door without saying anything.
You count who's inside that week.
There's a pen cap on the floor near the door.
The room has exceeded the cop.
And you're not sure when that happened.
The person in the room stops asking about the phone.
They ask when they can leave.
You're standing near the booking desk.
Somebody left a sandwich wrapper on the counter.
You already know who the child is calling about.
No one by that name was brought in tonight.
Something in your throat tightens - just for a second - and then you swallow and it's gone.
Because stepping forward means unwinding every absence you ever made.
Every name that was never entered.
You stand there a while longer.
Sees you turn left instead of right.
A week later someone mentions the room at the coffee machine.
The new cop says it back.
The new cop holds the door one night.
Holds it like something they've done a hundred times.
Two weeks later the new cop leads someone to the room.
You weren't there when it happened.
For a second you try to remember if you were ever that new - if there was a version of you that asked questions and waited for answers and believed the booking desk was the only way in.
You hear the new cop ask the question.
You don't remember teaching them that.
You don't remember learning it either.
They don't ask what's in the room.
You can't tell if you erased them or never entered them.
You've learned to think in absences.
You stopped asking a long time ago.
You don't remember if they were.
